coredata.dc:title: Influence of synthesis parameters on the optical properties of carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: García-Salcedo, Ángela J.
Giraldo-Pinto, Luz Ángela
Márquez-Castro, Deibys Josué
Tirado-Mejía, Liliana
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescent carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment from agricultural waste. Considering that the composition of the dots’ surface depends on the precursor source and that this composition influences the optical properties, this study focused on analyzing the effect of synthesis parameters on the formation of functional groups, on the core structure, on the morphology, and their influence on the absorption, excitation, emission, and quantum performance properties. It was obtained that by increasing the synthesis temperature, functional groups C = O, C = N, and CO were formed in the carbon dots obtained from the three precursors, and an additional group C = C in dots obtained from plantain peels, which are the only one that showed a degradation of the cellulose band. The absorption response indicated the formation of graphitic nuclei with oxygenated groups responsible for the red shift of the absorption maximum moving from 266 to 283 nm. The Raman response indicates that there are graphitic domains in the nucleus, where the defects band has a high intensity for the three precursors. The higher quantum yield value presented by the plantain dots is due to the surface, not the graphitic core. The spherical shape and size of around 2 nm were observed by TEM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrothermal treatment
Synthesis parameters
Quantum yield
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Vibrational modes obtained by infrared spectroscopy (FTIR)

We also thank the University of Quind\u00EDo for financial support, Project 1135.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the synthesis of carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment.
Absorption spectra in the mid-infrared of dry powder obtained from plantain peels (PP), orange peels (OP), and corn husks (CH). Dot lines show bands assigned to different functional groups.

Absorption spectra of CDs synthesized at three different temperatures during 20 h were obtained from corn husks (CH), orange peels (OP), and plantain peels (PP); the precursor's absorbance spectra are included to emphasize the influence of temperature.

Absorption spectra in the UV–vis range of CDs synthesized at three different temperatures and three carbonization times. CDs were obtained from corn husks (CH), orange peels (OP), and plantain peels (PP).
Emission spectra for CDs synthesized at 220 °C at different times, from (a) corn husks, (b) orange peels, and (c) plantain peels.

Description of the dependence of the maximum emission energy on the excitation energy for the three precursors and the nine synthesis conditions. The dot lines show the linear tendency and changes on the slope.

Energy values of excitation radiation in which the emission has a maximum intensity (upper graph) and energy values of the emission maxima (lower graph) for CDs obtained from corn husk, orange peels, and plantain peels synthesized at different temperatures and times.
Raman spectra of CDs synthesized at 220 °C and 160 °C for 15 h.
Micrograph of the carbon dots synthesized at 160 °C from CH (a), 220 °C from OP (b), and 220 °C from PP (c).
Quantum yield values exhibited by CD samples synthesized at different temperatures and times.
Average particle sizes measured from TEM images.

head.ce:title: Influence of synthesis parameters on the optical properties of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Grupo de Optoelectrónica, Instituto Interdisciplinario de las Ciencias, Universidad del Quindío, Cra 15 calle 12N Armenia, Quindío, Colombia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Grupo de Optoelectrónica, Instituto Interdisciplinario de las Ciencias, Universidad del Quindío, Cra 15 calle 12N Armenia, Quindío, Colombia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescent carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment from agricultural waste. Considering that the composition of the dots’ surface depends on the precursor source and that this composition influences the optical properties, this study focused on analyzing the effect of synthesis parameters on the formation of functional groups, on the core structure, on the morphology, and their influence on the absorption, excitation, emission, and quantum performance properties. It was obtained that by increasing the synthesis temperature, functional groups C = O, C = N, and CO were formed in the carbon dots obtained from the three precursors, and an additional group C = C in dots obtained from plantain peels, which are the only one that showed a degradation of the cellulose band. The absorption response indicated the formation of graphitic nuclei with oxygenated groups responsible for the red shift of the absorption maximum moving from 266 to 283 nm. The Raman response indicates that there are graphitic domains in the nucleus, where the defects band has a high intensity for the three precursors. The higher quantum yield value presented by the plantain dots is due to the surface, not the graphitic core. The spherical shape and size of around 2 nm were observed by TEM.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical, carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes less than 10 nm, with graphitic cores and functional groups on the surface [1,2]. CDs were first reported in 2004 by Xu et al. [3], obtained by purifying carbon nanotubes fabricated by arc discharge methods, and later by laser ablation of graphite powder and cement in 2006 [4]. Compared to semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CDs represent a superior alternative due to their properties.

They are biocompatible due to their high functionalization, which facilitates the interaction with a wide variety of biological systems. Unlike conventional fluorescent fluorochromes, CDs do not contain hard-to-degrade chains. Likewise, according to cytotoxicity research, CDs do not damage cell walls. Additionally, concerning their high solubility in water, it is an advantage that CDs can be incorporated into processes of biolabeling using a harmless medium such as water [5].

Therefore, CDs have attracted the attention of scientists due to their interesting applications in targeted sensing, bioimaging, biolabeling, and drug delivery [6]. For this reason, many synthesis methods have been developed, some cutting large carbon materials and others fusing small precursor molecules, corresponding to top-down or bottom-up synthesis, respectively.

CDs have been synthesized from plant material [7–9] showing that their optical behavior is associated with the composition of the precursor. However, the optical properties change with the method and the synthesis conditions. Very interesting studies have been reported on carbon dots associated with their fluorescent behavior, but to have control over these properties, it is necessary to do an in-depth study.

To contribute with a partial solution to the problem of agricultural waste management, bottom-up methods are used to synthesize CDs using plant sources since the organic molecules that make up these precursors can be carbonized at relatively low temperatures, forming CDs [10,11]. Likewise, this synthesis method is of great interest because of its low environmental impact, thus avoiding using expensive precursors and complex synthetization systems, strong acids, organic solvents, and oxidizing agents [12]. On the other hand, the different bottom-up synthesis methods are flexible and allow the synthesis of CDs with a wide range of optical properties and potential applications. The hydrothermal treatment is the most used because it is simple, economical, clean, and environmentally friendly. This is one of the preferred methods for CD synthesis in which the chemical transformation of the organic material is achieved by thermochemical degradation in water at high temperatures and pressure. Thus, the chemical composition on the surface and the photoluminescent properties of the CDs synthesized by this method depend on the precursor source and the synthesis parameters, temperature, and carbonization time used to obtain them. The study of the influence of synthesis conditions is of great importance for understanding the photoluminescence mechanisms of the resulting CDs [13].

In contrast to most of the research to date, focused on CD applications, this work reports the synthesis and the compositional, structure, and optical characterization of CDs obtained from agricultural waste: corn husks, orange peels, and plantain peels. The synthesis process was hydrothermal, in which temperature and carbonization time variations were carried out. The CD compositional and structural characterization were performed using FTIR spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy, respectively. Optical properties such as absorption, excitation and emission responses, and quantum yield were analyzed from spectrofluorometric and UV–visible spectroscopy measurements.

Corn, orange, and plantain were obtained from local markets. The peels of each precursor were washed and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 72 h. Then, the precursors were liquefied, passed through a 106 µm sieve, and the powder was subsequently packaged in polyethylene bags to be stored at room temperature until future use.

The CDs were obtained from three plant wastes: orange peels (OP-CD), plantain peels (PP-CD), and corn husks (CHCD) using the hydrothermal method. We solved the powder in water at 80 °C for 1 h. At room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at an RCF of 3420 xg for 20 min, and the supernatant obtained was subjected to hydrothermal treatment in a Teflon autoclave reactor. The treatment control parameters are temperature and time. When reaching room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at an RCF of 9500 xg for 20 min and the supernatant is the aqueous solution containing the carbon dots. This methodology is described graphically in Fig. 1 .

The response surface method was used to choose the hydrothermal synthesis parameters. The controlled factors were the precursor source (OP, PP, CH), the temperature (160, 190, and 220 °C), and the carbonization time (10, 15, and 20 h). The response variable was the fluorescence quantum yield (ϕ). The method chooses 15 parameter combinations. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated following an indirect method, in which ϕR is 54% as reported; the integrated luminescent intensity as a function of absorbance is compared between the CD sample and a reference sample, in this case, quinine sulfate, according to eq. (1). As these two parameters have a linear dependence, we calculate the slope for CD samples (mS ) and the slope for reference (mR ).
(1)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
(
m S
m R
)
(
η S
η R
)
2
η is the refractive index of solvents, water for samples (η =1.333) and H2SO4 at 0,1 M for reference solution (η =1.333).

The absorption response was obtained using a standard quartz cell on an Agilent Technologies 8453 UV–Visible spectrophotometer with tungsten and deuterium lamps. Excitation and emission spectra were measured on a Horiba Jobin Yvon FluoroMax Plus spectrofluorometer with a xenon lamp as the source in a 10 mm wide quartz cell (optical path). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was measured on a JASCO 4700 FTIR spectrophotometer with an ATR accessory with diamond crystal. The morphology of the carbon dots was characterized using a Jeol JEM-1011 transmission electron microscope (TEM) with an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. Raman spectroscopy of the carbon dots was performed using a HORIBA XploRA PLUS Raman spectrometer, with a 785 nm laser excitation and a 600 g/mm grating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Vibrational modes obtained by infrared spectroscopy (FTIR)

ce:section.ce:para: To determine the influence of the synthesis temperature on the presence of functional groups, FTIR spectroscopy was used in ATR mode (ATR-FTIR). The absorbance spectra of the agricultural residues, previously dried and pulverized, are presented in Fig. 2 , where the bands and their corresponding assignments are indicated, associated with the presence mainly of cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, and some proteins and lipids in fewer quantities [14]. The slight differences between the experimental results and some reported values are explained as the modifications in the vibrational energy due to the molecule's environment. The band centered at 3300 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching of the OH in the hydroxyl group; the band at 2964 cm-1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching of CH3; the bands at 2927 and 2850 cm-1 are associated to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of CH2 group, respectively; the band at 1730 cm-1 corresponds to the hemicellulose band of C = O stretching of carbonyl group [15]; the band around 1641 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching of C = C group on alkenes; the bands around 1600 and 1515 cm-1 are assigned to the stretching of the C = CC and C = CH groups of the aromatic rings, which are due to the presence of lignin in the precursor materials [16]; the band around 1457 cm-1 represents the bending vibration of the CH2 group; the band around 1425 cm-1 is assigned to OH flexure, and the band at 1375 cm-1 is assigned to CH2 symmetric flexure; the band around 1311 cm-1 represents the CN (aromatic amine) stretching vibration; the band at 1245 cm-1 corresponds to the symmetric stretching vibration of the CO-C group (lignin band) [15]; the bands around 1157 and 900 cm-1 are characteristic of cellulose [17] and correspond to the stretching vibration of the CO group and the deformation of the CH group, respectively; the band around 1010 cm-1 originates from the stretching of the CO group (primary alcohols), and the bands around 820 and 770 cm-1 are associated to out-of-plane bending vibrations (OPB) of the aromatic CH. Even though samples exhibit the presence of most of the mentioned functional groups, there are some differences in the line shape between the spectra associated with the precursor materials [14,18].

Starting from the compositional identification of the precursors' functional groups, their evolution in the CDs was studied by changing the synthesis temperature and synthesis time. In Fig. 3 , we present the absorption spectra in the mid-infrared range for the precursor and CD samples synthesized at 160, 190, and 220 °C for 20 h.

When comparing the CD spectra with the precursor's spectrum, four new bands appear, with higher definitions as the temperature increases. The bands in 1770 and 1707 cm-1 correspond to the stretching of C = O of carboxylic acids and esters, respectively. The bands around 1665 and 1199 cm-1 are associated with stretching the C = N group of amides and the stretching of the CO group of esters, respectively.

The presence of the first two mentioned bands is explained as follows. When the synthesis is carried out at 160 °C, the spectra exhibit the two C = O bands and the 1733 cm-1 hemicellulose band. This behavior is associated with the hydrolysis of a part of the hemicellulose [19], considering that the decomposition temperature is close to 180 °C. At 190 °C, the hemicellulose was hydrolyzed entirely, increasing the bands' intensity around 1770 and 1707 cm-1. The CO band is also attributed to the decomposition of hemicellulose. Similarly, the appearance of the other band during the hydrothermal carbonization of the precursor material, associated with the C = N bond vibration, is a consequence of the decomposition of macromolecules of the vegetal material containing nitrogen.

On the other hand, the increase in intensity of the bands located at 1600 and 1515 cm-1 assigned to the vibration of the aromatic rings reveals the aromatization process of the hydrolyzed products. This process can also be noted in the increase of the aromatic CH band around 820 and 770 cm-1.

In the particular case of the plantain peel, the band around 1025 cm-1 associated with the symmetric stretching of the CO-C group of the ethers diminishes in intensity as carbonization temperature increases. Simultaneously, the band at 1580 cm-1 associated with C = C increases with temperature. This behavior is explained as the modification of the CO-C group in the hydrolysis of cellulose, contributing to the formation of C = C cyclic groups. The increase of this bond means an increase in the aromatization process of the surface compounds.

It is worth mentioning that lignin hydrolysis requires higher temperatures than cellulose and hemicellulose; therefore, at 220 °C, only a small amount of the lignin decomposes [17]. This is why the characteristic bands of lignin (aromatic structure) are present in all spectra shown in Fig. 3.

These general absorbance characteristics in the infrared range are enhanced in samples treated at 220 °C. Instead, absorption spectra of samples synthesized at this temperature and during 10, 15, and 20 h do not exhibit significant changes. For the studied range of carbonization time, it does not impact the presence of functional groups.
These results agree with the processes reported in the hydrothermal synthesis method: hydrolysis, dehydration, aromatization, and carbonization processes occur.

We use the spectra of graphene and graphene oxide as a starting point to analyze the absorption spectra in the UV–vis range (Fig. 4 ). For graphene oxide, two absorption bands have been reported in the literature: the first at 230 nm and the second at 305 nm, associated withπ-π* orbital levels of C = C and n-π* of C = O, respectively [20-22]. For graphene, one absorption band has been reported at 266 nm [23].

The absorption spectra of the synthesized samples in the UV–vis range exhibit a small, intense band around 226 nm (π-π*) and a prominent band at 283 nm (n-π*). The relatively high intensity of the second band increases as the synthesis temperature increases; this means that this band, associated with the π-π* orbital levels, evidences the formation of graphitic domains in the CDs. The difference between the maxima´s wavelength values in pure samples (graphene and graphene oxide) and CDs is attributed to the transition energy between molecular orbitals of functional groups on the CD surface. It is also observed that for CHCD and OP-CD, 220 °C synthesis temperature favors the formation of graphitic domains, while in the case of PP-CD, even at 160 °C, the band emission associated with graphitic nuclei is obtained.

To study the CD luminescence mechanism, we measured the emission spectrum for different excitation wavelengths for all samples ((Iem
vs
. λem). In Fig. 5 , we show spectra for the three precursors calcinated at 220 °C and excitation wavelength in the range from 310 to 400 nm. For CD synthesis at 220 °C, the emission maxima are obtained with an excitation of 345 ± 5 nm. The excitation wavelength influences the emission maximum, not only on its wavelength but also on its intensity. This is a well-known behavior for CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal approach. They present a non-passivated surface and have a wide range of functional groups and, thus, multiple energetic states [24]. To analyze this dependence better, the energies of the emission maximum as a function of the excitation energies were plotted for the three different precursors, temperatures, and times (Fig. 6 ).

A linear dependence is observed for CHCD obtained at 160 °C and 190 °C. Linear behavior is associated with orbitals with energies in a continuum: as the excitation energy increases, the electrons move to high-energy states and become de-excited, emitting high-energy radiation. Transitions are made between orbitals with energies very close to each other, forming a band of states. A proportional response is obtained and observed as a linear behavior for all excitation energies. Since we do not see saturation energy, it may be that the gap has a higher energy and transitions are intraband-like; that is, the energetic separation between HOMO and LUMO has a higher value of energy than the excitation one. OP- CD obtained at 160 °C and 190 °C exhibit a breaking point: for higher energies, the emission energy tends to a saturation value, with a linear dependence of low slope. The response of OP-CD at 220 °C has three different slopes, but the one with the highest energy tends to have a saturation value. In PP-CD's case, a slope change is observed from a specific excitation energy, but there is no saturation value. The breakpoint is interpreted as the existence of a saturation energy for high excitation energy, beyond which there are no states with higher energy available to be occupied; that is, there is a gap (energy between HOMO and LUMO). There is no linear behavior, meaning distinct thin bands of states are available (Fig. 7 ).

This behavior is characteristic of CDs and is associated with the different surface functional groups [20,25,26]. We also observed that each precursor has a typical behavior of the emission maxima and presents dependence on the synthesis conditions. It is possible to tune λex and λem maxima, changing the synthesis parameters.

Additionally, the CDs of the three precursors synthesized at the highest temperature showed high photostability (Fig. S1), with minor decreases in photoluminescence intensity of 5.2 % for CH, 2.4 % for OP, and 5.3 % for PP when irradiated for three hours at maximum excitation. Likewise, measurements taken on different days present fluctuations of 6 % for PP, 4 % for CH, and 2 % for OP without showing a decreasing trend.

The CDs obtained from the three precursors used in this work are excited in the UV (from 3.4 to 3.8 eV) with emission in blue (from approximately 2.8 to 3.1 eV). The dispersion of the emission energy values decreases as the synthesis temperature increases, with PP being the one that requires less excitation energy to give a similar result to the other samples. A slight tendency to decrease the necessary excitation energies is observed when synthesizing at 220 °C. Meanwhile, CH requires a higher excitation energy even for points synthesized at 220 °C. These results are consistent with what was observed in the FTIR spectra, where the cellulose of PP degrades almost completely while that of CH requires more temperature to hydrolyze [19]. For OP, the behavior of the excitation energies and maximum emission is inverse; as the synthesis temperature increases, the excitation energy required to obtain maximum emission decreases. These results are translated into the quantum yield obtained using UV–vis absorption. In Table 1 , we present the results of these measurements. Although the quantum yield of CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method from organic wastes is known to be low, it can be seen that ϕ value increases when the synthesis temperature increases. OP at 160 °C and 10 h exhibits the lowest ϕ . PP synthesized at 220 °C, and 15 h produces CDs with the highest quantum performance.

This quantum yield is associated with forming larger quantities of C = C groups observed in FTIR due to the complete hydrolysis of PP cellulose. Consistent with the former description, increasing the synthesis temperature improves the CD performance, regardless of the precursor. In contrast, the effect of the synthesis time is insignificant, so carrying out very long synthesis treatments is unnecessary.

The quantum yield values obtained for these carbon dots are low compared to those obtained by other processes, such as enrichment with certain elements N, P, S, B, and Si, among others [27,28], or changing the solvent to ethanol, ionic liquids, or organic solvents [29]. However, there are reports [19,30] of quantum yield values similar to those we obtained using the hydrothermal synthesis.

Raman measurements were carried out to determine if there was an increase in the crystallinity of the nucleus of the carbon particles. It is observed that for all samples, the band associated with the crystalline component (G band) has a lower intensity than the defects band (D band) and does not increase with the synthesis temperature (Fig. 8 ). Therefore, the behavior observed previously in the absorption bands is associated with an increase in C = C bonds but not with an increase in the size of the crystallites; that is, although there is a formation of graphitic domains in the nucleus, the crystallinity does not increase. It is then confirmed that the name of the carbon points assigned to the synthesized particles corresponds to the reported definition.

Despite the similarity in the optical response of the carbon dots obtained from the different precursors, TEM microscopy images show that the distribution and size of the particles are different. Some representative images are presented in Fig. 9 . A wide size distribution was obtained for the CDs obtained from CH; Fig. 9.a shows the micrograph of a sample synthesized at 160 °C where some larger particles appear in addition to the nanoparticles. In Fig. 9.b, greater homogeneity in particle sizes is observed. And in Fig. 9.c the CDs form micelle-type geometries. The results of the statistics obtained from image analysis are presented in Table 2 .

We see that the synthesized CDs are nanoparticles with diameters lower than approximately 10 nm, and the size of the particles decreases in samples synthesized at higher temperatures (220 °C). The particles are dispersed in the solvent, and we concluded that these nanoparticles are water-soluble. Their shape is almost spherical.

Luminescent CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment from wastes of corn husks, orange peels, and plantain peels, a simple and environmentally friendly carbonization method. The CD optical properties are determined mainly by the precursor species and, in the second term, by the synthesis temperature, while no significant influence of time was observed. The FTIR absorption bands of the identified functional groups on the CD surface presented an evolution when the synthesis temperature increased. The CDs obtained from plantain peel are the only ones that showed more significant hydrolysis of cellulose and a formation of C = C bands associated with the conjugated cyclic groups.

Regarding the response obtained by UV–Vis absorption, the electronic transitions were related to the formation of graphitic domains in the core, which increased when the temperature increased. PP-CD at 220 °C exhibited the lowest intensity compared to the CDs from the other two precursors, but unlike them, the formation of graphitic domains was observed at 160 °C, the lowest synthesis temperature. Complementary to this, the Raman spectra allowed us to corroborate that there is no continuous crystalline network since the defect band (D) is more intense than the crystalline band (G). As no significant differences between precursors were observed in the nucleus's structure, the plantain precursor's greater efficiency was associated with the formation on the surface of conjugated cyclic functional groups. When excited in the ultraviolet, the dots of all three precursors emitted in blue; plantain dots require the least excitation energy. Likewise, the resulting CDs had an almost spherical morphology with an average diameter between 7 and 9 nm for the syntheses carried out at 160 °C and 2 nm for the syntheses carried out at 220 °C. In these TEM images, it is worth highlighting the micellar distribution of the carbon dots of the plantain precursor, which is probably related to the higher optical efficiency. These carbon dots are promising as inexpensive detection probes for microorganisms, requiring no additional surface treatment but selection by functional groups to assess selectivity.

Ángela J. García-Salcedo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Luz Ángela Giraldo-Pinto: Methodology, Investigation. Deibys Josué Márquez-Castro: Methodology, Investigation. Liliana Tirado-Mejía: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the funding from the Ministry of Science, Technology, and Innovation under call 891: Vocations and training in CTeI for economic reactivation in the framework of post-pandemic 2020, for the linking of young undergraduate and professional researchers and innovators in R + D + i projects, and under call 890: Strengthening CTeI in public higher education institutions, with code project: 1113–890–82054 and contract: ICETEX 2022–0778. We also thank the University of Quindío for financial support, Project 1135.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cartre.2024.100403.

J. Appl. Phys.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots based on natural resources: synthesis and applications in sensors
Microchem. J.
